transcription: Caller: Hello, hi. I am calling from Lyca Wellness. Recipient: From where? Caller: I am calling from Lyca Wellness, Nida. You have registered with us for upcoming workshop, na? To manage hormones, lifestyle, PCOS... Recipient: Oh, maybe. I don't remember. Maybe, yeah, I think so. Something to do with PCOS, no? Caller: Yes, so we have a workshop today at 6:30. I hope you're available to join our workshop at 6:30, right? Recipient: Yes, yes. Um, I'm still out actually. But I'll try to log in. I'll just try to listen whatever... yeah. Caller: Sure, no problem. Thank you. Recipient: Yeah, yeah. Thank you so much, yeah. Caller: Thank you.
corrected: Caller: Hello, I am calling from Lyca Wellness. You registered with us for an upcoming workshop on managing hormones, lifestyle, and PCOS, didn't you? Recipient: Oh, perhaps. I don't quite remember, but I think so. It was something to do with PCOS, right? Caller: Yes. We have a workshop today at 6:30 PM. I hope you are available to join us. Recipient: I am actually still out right now, but I will try to log in and listen to as much as I can. Caller: That's no problem at all. Thank you. Recipient: Thank you so much.
hindi: नमस्ते, मैं लाइका वेलनेस से बात कर रही हूँ। आपने हमारे आने वाले वर्कशॉप (हार्मोन और PCOS मैनेजमेंट) के लिए रजिस्टर किया था। क्या आपको याद है? / शायद, मुझे ठीक से याद नहीं है, लेकिन शायद हाँ। क्या यह PCOS से संबंधित था? / जी हाँ। आज शाम 6:30 बजे हमारी वर्कशॉप है। क्या आप शामिल हो पाएँगी? / मैं अभी बाहर हूँ, लेकिन मैं लॉग इन करके सुनने की पूरी कोशिश करूँगी। / कोई बात नहीं, धन्यवाद।
feedback: The speakers communicate effectively, but there are a few points for improvement. The use of 'na' at the end of sentences is a common Indianism; in formal English, it's better to use 'right?' or 'haven't you?'. The recipient uses 'whatever' at the end of a sentence, which sounds incomplete; 'whatever I can' or 'as much as possible' would be better. The pronunciation is clear, and the flow is natural for a beginner-intermediate level.
